footballers.name: Wes Morgan
clues: I was born on the 21st of January 1984. I played 30 times for my country, never scoring a goal.
I'm the first Jamaican ever to win a Premier League medal — and the first to score in a Champions League game (against Sevilla in 2017).
I spent ten years at Nottingham Forest before transferring up the road.
I was born in Nottingham, but I switched my international allegiance from England's youth ranks to Jamaica in 2013 — and captained them to the 2015 Gold Cup final.
I captained Leicester City to the Premier League title in 2015–16 — and played every single minute of all 38 games.

footballers.name: Diego Godín
clues: I was born on the 16th of February 1986. I played 161 times for my country, scoring 8 goals.
I'm one of only six players ever to score in finals of both major European trophies — the Champions League and the Europa League.
I'm godfather to Antoine Griezmann's daughter — when France played us in the 2018 World Cup quarter-final, he didn't celebrate his goal out of respect.
I'm Uruguay's all-time most-capped player.
I scored a header against Barcelona on the final day of La Liga 2013–14 to win Atlético Madrid their first title in 18 years.

footballers.name: Michail Antonio
clues: I was born on the 28th of March 1990. I have 5 goals from 24 appearances for my country — but not my country of birth.
I came through non-league football, starting out at Tooting & Mitcham before Reading signed me.
I once recreated the famous Dirty Dancing lift after scoring against Arsenal — using a cardboard cutout of myself.
I became my club's all-time Premier League top scorer in August 2021, replacing the previous holder who was himself an iconic Italian striker.
I won the Europa Conference League in 2023 — my club's first trophy in 43 years.

footballers.name: Dušan Tadić
clues: I was born on the 20th of November 1988. I played 111 times for my country, scoring 23 goals.
After my display in a 4–1 Champions League win at the Bernabéu in 2019, I became only the ninth player ever to receive a 10/10 rating from L'Équipe.
I captained my country at the 2022 World Cup.
I was the 2020–21 Dutch Footballer of the Year and Ajax Player of the Year.
After four years at Southampton I joined Ajax — and tore Real Madrid apart in the Champions League with a goal and two assists.

footballers.name: Phil Jones
clues: I was born on the 21st of February 1992. I played 27 times for my country, never scoring a goal.
After a serious injury in 2019 I spent 20 months sidelined — described it as the lowest I've ever felt in my life.
I was once described by Sir Alex Ferguson in 2013: 'The way he is looking, he could be our best ever player.'
I won 4 major trophies at Manchester United including a Premier League and a Europa League.
I became a running joke for football's social media age — particularly for my contorted facial expressions.

footballers.name: Eidur Gudjohnsen
clues: I was born on the 15th of September 1978. I played 78 times for my country, scoring 26 goals.
In my first Premier League season I scored 19 goals, forming a strike partnership with Michael Ricketts.
I won back-to-back league titles in two different countries, lifting the trophy in England and then in Spain.
My father and I made history together — he was substituted off and I came on in the same international match in 1996.
I won the Champions League in 2009.

footballers.name: Pablo Hernández
clues: I was born on the 11th of April 1985. I played 4 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
Before coming to the Premier League I played most of my career in La Liga.
I arrived in the Premier League after being signed by Michael Laudrup on loan from my Spanish club.
A manager who had previously coached in Argentina's top flight transformed my career in the Championship.
I won Player of the Season at this club when we got promoted.

footballers.name: Diego Simeone
clues: I was born on the 28th of April 1970. I played 106 times for my country, scoring 11 goals.
During my career I played alongside Diego Maradona.
I won the UEFA Cup with a Milan club and the domestic double with a Rome-based club.
I won a UEFA Cup and a league-and-cup double at the same Spanish club.
At the 1998 World Cup, I was involved in one of football's most infamous incidents.

footballers.name: Dominic Solanke
clues: I was born on the 14th of September 1997. I have been capped 5 times at senior level, scoring 0 goals. My first appearance came against Brazil in 2017.
I won the Golden Ball at the U-20 World Cup in South Korea, finishing as the tournament's best player.
I came through an academy in west London but never made a league appearance for the first team, leaving on a free transfer.
I scored just one Premier League goal in two seasons on Merseyside.
I scored 19 Championship goals to win promotion, before leaving for around £65 million.

footballers.name: Gabriel Batistuta
clues: I was born on the 1st of February 1969. I played 78 times for my country, scoring 54 goals.
I started my career in Argentina at Newell's Old Boys and River Plate before moving to Europe at the age of 22.
I hold my country's record for the most hat-tricks at World Cup finals, and I scored in three consecutive tournaments.
I spent nine years at a club in Tuscany known for its purple shirts, becoming their all-time top scorer — and I cried when they were relegated.
I finally won the Scudetto when I moved to another Italian club in 2000.

footballers.name: Adel Taarabt
clues: I was born on the 24th of May 1990. I have 4 goals from 30 appearances for my country — but not my country of birth.
I joined a north London club's academy as a teenager but managed just two league appearances for their first team.
A manager once said the only way he could get any weight off me was when I had tonsillitis.
I won the Championship, finishing as the league's Player of the Season.
I won the league title in Portugal with one of the country's biggest clubs.
footballers.story: A mercurial talent who could dribble past entire defences on his day but rarely produced it consistently enough to satisfy his managers. Taarabt arrived at Tottenham as a teenager from Lens but never broke through, finding his feet instead at QPR where he was the Championship's standout performer in 2010–11. His close control and audacious skill made him a fans' favourite, but his attitude was questioned at every stop. A loan to AC Milan offered a glimpse of what might have been, before he settled at Benfica where he finally found stability, winning multiple league titles and becoming a reliable squad player into his thirties.
didYouKnow: He once nutmegged three players in a single run during a Championship match against Bristol City
He represented both France and Morocco at youth level before committing to Morocco
Harry Redknapp called him the best player in the Championship by a mile during QPR's promotion season

footballers.name: Antonio Di Natale
clues: I was born on the 13th of October 1977. I scored 11 goals in 42 appearances for my country.
I spent almost my entire career in Italian football.
I didn't score my first Serie A goal until I was 24, and didn't become a regular starter until I was nearly 30.
I won the Serie A top scorer award twice in consecutive seasons, despite playing for a club that never challenged for the title.
I turned down a move to one of Italy's biggest clubs because my family had fallen in love with life in Friuli.
footballers.story: A one-club legend in Serie A's truest sense. Di Natale spent 12 years at Udinese, becoming the club's all-time top scorer and one of the most prolific strikers in Italian football history. Small in stature but explosive in the box, he hit his peak remarkably late — scoring 29 league goals at the age of 33 in 2010–11, finishing as Serie A's top scorer for two consecutive seasons. He turned down moves to Juventus and other giants out of loyalty to Udinese, retiring as one of the most beloved figures in Italian football.
didYouKnow: He scored 227 goals for Udinese, a record that may never be broken
He turned down a move to Juventus in 2011 when he was the top scorer in Serie A
He scored against every single Serie A team during his career

footballers.name: Papiss Cissé
clues: I was born on the 3rd of June 1985. I scored 16 goals in approximately 30 appearances for my country.
I scored 13 goals in my first 12 Premier League appearances after joining in January.
I wore the number 9 shirt at my English club — previously worn by the club's all-time record scorer.
In the Bundesliga, I scored 22 goals in a single season — a record for an African player at the time.
I scored a goal from 37 yards with the outside of my foot against Chelsea that won BBC Goal of the Season.
footballers.story: Cissé arrived at Newcastle in January 2012 as a relatively unknown quantity from Freiburg — and promptly scored 13 goals in 14 games to finish the season, including two of the most spectacular strikes the Premier League has ever seen, both in the same match against Chelsea. His curling, physics-defying effort from outside the box at Stamford Bridge is still regularly voted one of the greatest goals in league history. Though injuries and inconsistency followed, that electric first half-season cemented him as a Tyneside legend.
honours: Newcastle United Player of the Year nominee (2012)
didYouKnow: His goal against Chelsea at Stamford Bridge in 2012 won the Premier League Goal of the Season
He scored 13 goals in his first 14 Premier League appearances — one of the best debut half-seasons ever
He initially wore the number 9 shirt at Newcastle, a shirt with enormous pressure and history at the club

footballers.name: Fernandinho
clues: I was born on the 4th of May 1985. I scored 2 goals in 53 appearances for my country.
I started my country's most humiliating World Cup defeat on home soil — and was substituted at half-time.
I've played as a defensive midfielder, central midfielder, and centre-back at the highest level.
I spent eight years in the Ukrainian Premier League before moving to England.
I won five Premier League titles with the same club.
footballers.story: The quiet enforcer behind Manchester City's dominance. Fernandinho arrived from Shakhtar Donetsk in 2013 and spent a decade at the Etihad, winning five Premier League titles and becoming one of the most reliable midfielders in the league's history. Never the headline act alongside the likes of Silva, De Bruyne and Agüero, he was the player Guardiola trusted most to control the tempo and do the dirty work. He captained the side in his final seasons, a testament to his leadership and professionalism.
didYouKnow: He won more League Cups (6) than any other competition during his career
He played over 350 games for Manchester City across 9 seasons
He was named Manchester City captain for the 2020–21 season at the age of 35

footballers.name: Brad Friedel
clues: I was born on the 18th of May 1971. I played 82 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
I signed for a club on Merseyside but struggled to break into the first team, making only a handful of appearances.
I once scored from a corner kick in the 90th minute — one of only a handful of goalkeepers to score in the Premier League.
A manager who first worked with me in Turkey later signed me for an English club, where I became first choice for nearly a decade.
I hold the all-time record for the most consecutive Premier League appearances — over 300 in a row.
footballers.story: The most successful American goalkeeper in Premier League history. Friedel set a then-record of 310 consecutive Premier League appearances between 2004 and 2012, playing for Blackburn, Aston Villa and Tottenham without missing a single league game for over eight years. A commanding presence in goal with outstanding shot-stopping ability, he was a model of consistency in an era when American players in the English top flight were a rarity. He also famously scored a goal for Blackburn in the League Cup.
honours: 310 consecutive Premier League appearances (record at the time)
didYouKnow: He played in three World Cups for the United States (1998, 2002, 2006)
He scored a last-minute equaliser for Blackburn against Charlton in the 2004 League Cup
He was still playing Premier League football at the age of 42

footballers.name: Andriy Shevchenko
clues: I was born on the 29th of September 1976. I played 111 times for my country, scoring 48 goals.
I won the Ballon d’Or after years of prolific goalscoring at the highest level.
I won five league titles and one Champions League during my career in Italian football.
I won the 2003 Champions League final, scoring the winning penalty in a shootout against Juventus at Old Trafford.
I moved to England for big money but the transfer proved unsuccessful — I never came close to matching my prolific form from Italy.
footballers.story: One of the deadliest strikers of his generation. Shevchenko burst onto the scene at Dynamo Kyiv before becoming the focal point of a fearsome AC Milan attack for eight glorious seasons. His pace, movement and clinical finishing brought him the Ballon d'Or in 2004 and over 170 goals for the Rossoneri. The move to Chelsea for £30 million never worked — he looked a shadow of his former self in England — but his legacy in Italian football is untouchable. He later managed Ukraine at Euro 2020, guiding them to the quarter-finals.
didYouKnow: He scored the winning penalty in the 2003 Champions League final against Juventus
He is AC Milan's second all-time top scorer with 175 goals
He grew up in Kyiv and was five years old when the Chernobyl disaster occurred less than 100km away

footballers.name: Luke Shaw
clues: I was born on the 12th of July 1995. I have over 30 caps for my country, scoring 1 goal.
I moved for a fee of around £30 million as a teenager, making me the most expensive teenager in world football at the time.
I had a well-documented difficult relationship with José Mourinho, who publicly criticised my fitness and commitment multiple times.
I came through the academy at Southampton before moving to one of the biggest clubs in England at the age of 18.
I started for England throughout Euro 2020 (played in 2021), including the final at Wembley where my country lost on penalties to Italy.
footballers.story: Shaw burst onto the scene at Southampton as one of the most exciting young full-backs in English football, earning a £30 million move to Manchester United at just 18 — making him the most expensive teenager in world football at the time. A horrific double leg break against PSV in 2015 threatened his career, but he fought back. His peak came at Euro 2020, where he scored the opening goal against Italy in the final within two minutes. His relationship with Mourinho became one of football's most public player-manager feuds, but Shaw consistently proved his quality when fit and confident.
didYouKnow: He was the youngest player to appear for Southampton in the Premier League, debuting at 16
His goal against Italy at Euro 2020 was the fastest ever scored in a European Championship final
He was publicly criticised by José Mourinho on multiple occasions, including being told he only played well when Mourinho picked the team for him

footballers.name: Antonio Cassano
clues: I was born on the 12th of July 1982. I played 39 times for my country, scoring 10 goals.
I’m one of very few players to have played for both Milan clubs — starting at one and crossing the city to the other.

I was as famous for my waistline as my talent — I once reportedly gained 14 kilos between seasons. Managers struggled to control me, and my fallouts with coaches became as legendary as my ability on the ball.
I won La Liga with Real Madrid and the Serie A title with AC Milan.
My former coach coined a word after me — “cassanata” — which Italian journalists still use today as a byword for any behaviour incompatible with team spirit.
footballers.story: Arguably the most naturally gifted Italian footballer of his generation — and almost certainly the most frustrating. Cassano emerged as a teenage sensation at Bari, earning a move to Roma where his outrageous skill thrilled the Stadio Olimpico. But everywhere he went, controversy followed: weight problems, arguments with coaches, and a self-destructive streak that prevented him from fulfilling his extraordinary potential. He won titles at Real Madrid and AC Milan but was never a regular at either. A stroke at 29 nearly ended his life, yet he returned to play on for several more years. His talent was never in doubt — only his temperament.
didYouKnow: His former coach Fabio Capello coined the term "cassanata" to describe behaviour incompatible with team spirit — the word is still used by Italian journalists
He reportedly gained 14 kilos between seasons on more than one occasion
He suffered a stroke during a flight with AC Milan in 2011 but made a full recovery and returned to football within months

footballers.name: David Trezeguet
clues: I was born on the 15th of October 1977. I played 71 times for my country, scoring 34 goals.
I formed a striking partnership with Thierry Henry at my first European club, winning two league titles.
I won both a World Cup and a European Championship with my country within two years.
I scored the golden goal in extra time of a major international final — beating Italy with a volley into the roof of the net.
When my club was relegated after a match-fixing scandal, I was one of the few stars who stayed to help them return.
footballers.story: Born in France to Argentine parents, Trezeguet was one of the most lethal finishers in European football during the early 2000s. He scored the golden goal that won France Euro 2000 and went on to become Juventus's all-time top foreign scorer, netting over 170 goals in a decade in Turin. A pure poacher with razor-sharp instincts in the box, he remained loyal to Juventus even after their relegation to Serie B in 2006, helping them win promotion back to the top flight. His missed penalty in the 2006 World Cup final against Italy remains one of football's most heartbreaking moments.
didYouKnow: He scored the golden goal in the Euro 2000 final against Italy — a left-footed volley in extra time
He is Juventus's all-time top foreign scorer with 171 goals
He stayed with Juventus in Serie B after the Calciopoli scandal when most stars left, earning him legendary status among Juve fans

footballers.name: Danny Rose
clues: I was born on the 2nd of July 1990. I played 29 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
I started a Champions League final — but finished on the losing side.
I was converted from a winger to a full-back, a decision that led to me playing in a World Cup semi-final.
I came through an academy in Yorkshire before moving to London as a teenager.
On my league debut, I scored a long-range volley in a derby match that won Goal of the Season.
footballers.story: Rose announced himself to the world with a thunderous 30-yard volley on his Tottenham debut against Arsenal in 2010 — a goal that won Match of the Day's Goal of the Season. After loan spells at various clubs, he established himself as Spurs' first-choice left-back under Pochettino and earned regular England caps. He was part of the Tottenham side that reached the 2019 Champions League final. Rose was also one of the first high-profile players to speak publicly about his struggles with depression, helping to break the stigma around mental health in football.
didYouKnow: His debut goal against Arsenal — a dipping volley from nearly 30 yards — won the 2009–10 Goal of the Season
He spoke openly about suffering from depression in 2019, becoming one of the first active Premier League players to do so
He was racially abused while playing for England in Serbia in 2012, an incident that led to sanctions against the Serbian FA

footballers.name: Jan Koller
clues: I was born on the 30th of March 1973. I played 91 times for my country, scoring 55 goals.
I started my career as a goalkeeper before being converted to a striker.
I won the Bundesliga in my first season at a German club.
At 2.02 metres tall, I was one of the tallest outfield players in top-level European football.
I scored in the 2002 UEFA Cup final for Borussia Dortmund, but we lost 3–2.
footballers.story: Two metres and two centimetres of Czech striker. "Dino." Borussia Dortmund's aerial nightmare, the Czech Republic's all-time top scorer, and — somehow — a former goalkeeper who once kept a clean sheet between the sticks for Dortmund mid-match.
footballers.numbers: 199 club goals · 55 in 91 for the Czech Republic (still the record)
didYouKnow: Went in goal for Dortmund in 2002 after their keeper was sent off and all subs were used — kept a clean sheet for the remaining 23 minutes and was named the Bundesliga's goalkeeper of the week.
Started his career as a goalkeeper before being converted to striker — explains the in-game cameo.
Stood 2.02m / 6'8" — universally nicknamed "Dino" because he was taller than the dressing room.

footballers.name: Emile Heskey
clues: I was born on the 11th of January 1978. I played 62 times for my country, scoring 7 goals.
Scored on my England debut against Hungary in April 1999.
Moved to Liverpool for £11 million in 2000, winning a cup treble in my first season.
Had spells at Birmingham City, Wigan Athletic and Aston Villa after leaving Anfield.
Known for my physical strength and unselfish hold-up play, I was a favourite strike partner for Michael Owen.

footballers.name: Lukas Podolski
clues: I was born on the 4th of June 1985. I played 130 times for my country, scoring 49 goals — the third-highest tally in their history.
I won the Best Young Player award at the 2006 World Cup, hosted in my adopted home country.
I spent three years at Bayern Munich but was never a regular starter, often playing second fiddle to the likes of Ribéry and Robben.
I spent three seasons in the Premier League, known for my powerful left-footed shooting but less so for my work rate off the ball.
I scored a thunderbolt against Montpellier on my Arsenal home debut and later netted a famous winner against Bayern Munich in the Champions League at the Emirates.

footballers.name: Chris Sutton
clues: I was born on the 10th of March 1973. I played once for my country, scoring 0 goals.
Signed for a club record £5 million fee, making me one of the most expensive British players at the time.
Scored in a famous 3–2 Champions League win over Juventus at Celtic Park in 2001.
Won the Premier League title in 1994–95 as part of the famous SAS strike partnership.
Now a well-known football pundit, regularly appearing on BBC Radio 5 Live and Match of the Day.

footballers.name: Nigel Winterburn
clues: I was born on the 11th of December 1963. I played 3 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
Won three league titles and two FA Cups during a 13-year spell in English football.
Signed from Wimbledon in 1987 by George Graham — I went on to win both the old First Division and the Premier League with the same club.
I finished my career at West Ham United, playing until the age of 38 — one of the oldest outfield players in the Premier League at the time.
I was part of one of the most elite defences in Premier League history, alongside Adams, Bould and Dixon.

footballers.name: Yakubu Aiyegbeni
clues: I was born on the 22nd of November 1982. I played 58 times for my country, scoring 21 goals.
I scored seven goals in eight Champions League matches for an Israeli club — including a hat-trick against Olympiakos.
David Moyes signed me for a club-record fee in 2007 — I scored 21 goals in all competitions in my first season.
I scored all four goals in a 4–2 Premier League win in December 2011.
I scored 25 Premier League goals in 82 appearances for a club on Merseyside.
footballers.story: The Yak. Built like a wardrobe, finished like a fox. Quietly one of the Premier League's most efficient strikers of his era — Portsmouth, Boro, Everton — racking up goals while half the league forgot how good he actually was.
footballers.numbers: 170+ league goals across his career · 21 in 58 for Nigeria
didYouKnow: Scored seven in eight Champions League games for Maccabi Haifa in 2002–03 — including a hat-trick against Olympiakos — which earned him his Portsmouth move.
Scored all four in Blackburn's 4–2 win over Swansea in December 2011 — his first four-goal haul since the Pompey days.
Played barefoot on the streets of Benin City until his brother bought him his first pair of boots aged 12.

footballers.name: Andrey Arshavin
clues: I was born on the 29th of May 1981. I scored 17 goals in 75 appearances for my country.
I led my club — not an English one — to a UEFA Cup and Super Cup double, winning both finals in 2008.
I moved to London, where I played from 2009 to 2013.
At Euro 2008, I was named in the Team of the Tournament after reaching the semi-finals — but we lost to the eventual champions.
I scored four goals in a single away match at Anfield — a 4–4 draw that is still considered one of the greatest Premier League games ever played.

footballers.name: Michu
clues: I was born on the 21st of March 1986. I played once for my country, scoring 0 goals.
I scored 22 goals in my debut Premier League season.
I won the 2013 Football League Cup, scoring in the final.
The following season I managed just two Premier League goals — and within three years, a chronic ankle injury forced me to retire at 31.
My club was Swansea City.
footballers.story: One season of pure magic. 22 goals for newly-promoted Swansea, the best £2m signing in Premier League history, the player every Sunday-league team wanted to compare themselves to. Then his ankle gave out and he was gone — football's most beautiful and brief shooting star.
footballers.numbers: 22 goals in his Premier League debut season · 83 club goals total
didYouKnow: Bought by Swansea for around £2m from Rayo Vallecano — widely considered one of the best-value Premier League signings ever.
Bought shares in his boyhood club Real Oviedo in 2012 alongside Cazorla and Mata to help them avoid financial collapse.
Forced into early retirement at 31 by chronic ankle problems — said in 2022 that he has "the ankle of a 90-year-old" despite multiple operations.

footballers.name: Christian Vieri
clues: I was born on the 12th of July 1973. I played 49 times for my country, scoring 23 goals.
In 1999 I became the world's most expensive player.
I played for seven different clubs in Serie A — more than almost any other top striker of my generation.
I grew up in Sydney and idolised the Australian cricketer Allan Border — I'd have preferred to be a professional cricketer.
I scored nine goals across two World Cups — and spent six years at Inter Milan.
footballers.story: A nomadic Italian striker raised in Sydney who became the world's most expensive player when Inter signed him for €49m in 1999. Bullish, two-footed, devastating in the air — defined the Inter attack of the early 2000s alongside Ronaldo. Played for seven Serie A clubs, never quite settling, but scored everywhere he went.
footballers.numbers: 194 club goals · 23 in 49 for Italy · 4 goals at the 2002 World Cup
didYouKnow: Grew up in Sydney and wanted to be a cricketer. Idolised Allan Border.
Won a Ferrari off Atlético president Jesús Gil for a hat-trick against PAOK — never collected it because he felt guilty about leaving the club a year later.
Successfully sued Inter and Telecom Italia in 2012 after discovering owner Massimo Moratti had him under phone surveillance.

footballers.name: Eric Abidal
clues: I was born on the 11th of September 1979. I played 67 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
I won three consecutive league titles with a club in central-eastern France before moving to Spain.
I won four league titles and two Champions League medals with the same Spanish club.
I could play both left-back and centre-back at the highest level.
I later became sporting director at the club where I'd won the Champions League.

footballers.name: Fabricio Coloccini
clues: I was born on the 22nd of January 1982. I played 39 times for my country, scoring 1 goal.
I made my professional debut at one of Argentina's most famous clubs — but played just one league match before being sold to Italy.
I made over 275 appearances across eight seasons at my English club — my longest stint anywhere.
I experienced relegation with my English club in my first season — but stayed and helped them win promotion as champions.
I was named in the PFA Team of the Year in 2012 as a centre-back at a north-east English club.

footballers.name: Wes Brown
clues: I was born on the 13th of October 1979. I played 23 times for my country, scoring 1 goal.
I was sent off in 2015 for a foul that John O'Shea had actually committed — the red card was rescinded on appeal but the moment became one of the strangest dismissals in Premier League history.
I won the Jimmy Murphy Young Player of the Year award twice as a Manchester United youth — only Ryan Giggs had done it before me.
Sir Alex Ferguson once called me 'without question the best natural defender this club has had for years'.
I spent fifteen years at Manchester United — five Premier League titles, two Champions Leagues.
footballers.story: Manchester United's quiet, two-footed Mancunian — Sir Alex's go-to fill-in defender for over a decade. Five league titles, two Champions Leagues, never the headline act, always there when needed. The kind of squad player every great team is built on.
honours: 13 trophies in total at Old Trafford
footballers.numbers: 6 career goals · 23 caps for England
didYouKnow: Sir Alex Ferguson once said Brown was "without question the best natural defender this club has had for years" — high praise for a player whose career was largely about quiet reliability.
Won the Jimmy Murphy Young Player of the Year award twice as a Manchester United youth — only Ryan Giggs had previously done it.

Was sent off in 2015 for a foul his Sunderland teammate John O'Shea had actually committed — the red card was rescinded on appeal but the moment became one of the strangest dismissals in Premier League history.

footballers.name: Ray Parlour
clues: I was born on the 7th of March 1973. I played 10 times for my country, never scoring a goal.
My nickname 'The Romford Pelé' was an ironic joke first cracked by Marc Overmars when he joined Arsenal — and it stuck for the rest of my career.
I scored a 30-yarder against Finland in 2000 that clearly crossed the line — wrongly disallowed by a linesman in one of the era's most notorious refereeing errors.
I opened the 2002 FA Cup final with a 30-yard screamer — prompting Tim Lovejoy's now-legendary commentary line: 'Oh, it's all right, it's only Ray Parlour.'
I won three Premier League titles and four FA Cups across twelve years at Arsenal.
footballers.story: The Romford Pelé. Twelve years at Arsenal, three Premier League titles, four FA Cups — Parlour was the engine room of the Wenger years that nobody outside the squad ever quite gave proper credit to. Ran further than anyone, never complained, scored a 30-yarder in an FA Cup final.
footballers.numbers: 32 club goals · 10 caps for England
didYouKnow: Opened the 2002 FA Cup final with a 30-yard screamer — prompting Tim Lovejoy's now-legendary commentary line: "Oh, it's all right, it's only Ray Parlour."
Scored a 30-yard strike against Finland in 2000 that clearly crossed the line — wrongly disallowed by a linesman in one of the era's most notorious refereeing errors.
His nickname "the Romford Pelé" was self-deprecating, originally coined as a joke by Marc Overmars — and stuck for the rest of his career.

footballers.name: Fernando Llorente
clues: I was born on the 26th of February 1985. I played 24 times for my country, scoring 7 goals.
I scored the winner against Manchester City in the 2019 Champions League quarter-final — but only got 8 minutes of the final against Liverpool.
After a defeat to Real Sociedad in 2012 I had a furious argument with manager Marcelo Bielsa and walked out of training — it ended my Athletic Bilbao career.
I won Euro 2012 with Spain as an unused squad member — and the World Cup in 2010 as a fringe player.
I was Athletic Bilbao's towering number 9 — and ended my career as Tottenham and Napoli's most physically improbable super-sub.
footballers.story: El Rey León. Athletic Bilbao's towering Basque number 9 in the Marcelo Bielsa era — all elbows, headers and physical authority. Won everything with Spain's golden generation, won Serie A at Juventus, and ended his career as Tottenham's most physically improbable super-sub.
footballers.numbers: 151 club goals · 7 in 24 for Spain
didYouKnow: Born in Pamplona but qualified for Athletic Bilbao through his Navarrese roots — stayed at the club until he was 28 before finally leaving for Juventus.
Got just 8 minutes of the 2019 Champions League final against Liverpool — his second European final cameo (also lost the 2015 final with Juventus).
Reinvented himself as a late-career super-sub at Spurs and Napoli — defenders simply couldn't deal with his aerial presence in the last 20 minutes.

footballers.name: Paul Konchesky
clues: I was born on the 15th of May 1981. I played 2 times for my country, never scoring a goal.
I became Charlton's youngest first-team player at 16 years and 93 days, against Oxford United in 1997.
My disastrous Liverpool spell is remembered for an error against Spurs that gifted Aaron Lennon the winning goal — and an unprintable Facebook reaction from my mum.
I scored in West Ham's 2006 FA Cup final against Liverpool — they won on penalties.
I now run a pie and mash café in Brentwood called Konch's Kafe — with my mum on the staff.
footballers.story: The East End left-back of the early 2000s — a Charlton academy product who somehow ended up at Liverpool in the absolute worst-managed transfer window of the Roy Hodgson era. Two England caps. One pie shop. No nonsense.
footballers.numbers: 14 club goals · 2 England caps
didYouKnow: Was Charlton's youngest ever first-team player at 16 years and 93 days — a record later broken by Jonjo Shelvey.
His brief Liverpool spell is remembered for an error against Spurs that gifted Aaron Lennon the winner — and a famously unhappy public reaction from his mother on Facebook.
Opened a pie and mash café in Brentwood in 2015, with his mum on the staff — the most East End post-football career imaginable.

footballers.name: Sylvain Distin
clues: I was born on the 16th of December 1977. I never played for my country, never scoring a goal.
I was approached to play for Guadeloupe at the 2011 CONCACAF Gold Cup but turned it down to focus on Everton's preseason.
I assisted Tim Howard's 100-yard goal in 2011–12 — only the fourth goal scored from open play by a goalkeeper in Premier League history.
I made over 450 Premier League appearances — more than any other foreign outfielder in the competition's history.
I famously announced my 'international retirement' on Twitter in 2014 — having never won a single France cap.
footballers.story: The Premier League's most-capped foreign outfielder — and yet not a single France cap to show for it. Manchester City, Portsmouth, Everton — calm, dominant in the air, the kind of defender every relegation-threatened side wished they had. The best French centre-half never to play for France.
footballers.numbers: 402+ Premier League appearances (most by any foreign outfielder) · 0 France caps
didYouKnow: Despite playing more Premier League games than any other foreign outfielder, he was never picked by France — announced his "international retirement" on Twitter in 2014, deadpan.
Was approached to represent Guadeloupe at the 2011 CONCACAF Gold Cup but turned it down to focus on Everton's preseason.
Assisted Tim Howard's freak 100-yard goal in 2011–12 — only the fourth keeper-from-open-play goal in Premier League history.

footballers.name: Niko Kranjčar
clues: I was born on the 13th of August 1984. I played 81 times for my country, scoring 15 goals.
My move from Dinamo Zagreb to bitter rivals Hajduk Split in 2005 was so divisive that 200 candles were lit in mourning outside my home — and 10,000 fans showed up at my Hajduk unveiling.
I am the son of Zlatko, who coached our country to the 1998 World Cup semi-final.
I won the FA Cup with Portsmouth in 2007–08.
I followed Harry Redknapp to three different clubs — Portsmouth, Tottenham and QPR.
footballers.story: The silky Croatian playmaker who followed Harry Redknapp around for a decade — Portsmouth, Tottenham, QPR, three different clubs, three different reunions. Hailed at home as "the next Prosinečki". Never quite delivered in the Premier League, but moments of class were never in doubt.
footballers.numbers: 61 club goals · 81 caps for Croatia
didYouKnow: His move from Dinamo Zagreb to bitter rivals Hajduk Split in 2005 was so divisive that 200 candles were lit in mourning outside his home — and 10,000 fans showed up for his Hajduk unveiling.
Joined up with Harry Redknapp at three different clubs (Portsmouth, Tottenham, QPR) — one of the great manager-player Premier League marriages.
Comes from football royalty — his father Zlatko coached Croatia to the 1998 World Cup semi-final.

footballers.name: Benni McCarthy
clues: I was born on the 12th of November 1977. I played 79 times for my country, scoring 32 goals.
Manchester United hired me as their first-team striker's coach from 2022 to 2024.
I scored four goals in 13 minutes against Namibia at AFCON 1998 — became joint top scorer of the tournament and Player of the Tournament.
I spent four seasons at Blackburn Rovers — finished second in the 2006–07 Premier League Golden Boot race with 18 goals.
I'm the only South African ever to win the Champions League — did it with Mourinho's Porto in 2003–04.
footballers.story: South Africa's all-time top scorer, a Champions League winner with Mourinho's Porto, and the man who gave Blackburn Rovers a centre-forward of genuine pedigree for four seasons. Spiky, confident, deadly when he was on it.
footballers.numbers: 32 international goals (South Africa's all-time top scorer)
didYouKnow: The only South African ever to win the Champions League — scored crucial goals against Manchester United in Porto's run.
Scored four goals in 13 minutes against Namibia at AFCON 1998, en route to sharing the tournament's Golden Boot and winning Player of the Tournament.
Won league titles in three different countries on two continents (Netherlands, Portugal, South Africa) — a first for a South African footballer.

footballers.name: Mark Schwarzer
clues: I was born on the 6th of October 1972. I played 109 times for my country, never scoring a goal.
I won the Premier League with Leicester in 2015–16 as a squad keeper — the first player since Eric Cantona to win it with two clubs in successive seasons.
At 41 years and 65 days I became the oldest player ever to debut in the Champions League — keeping a clean sheet for Chelsea against Steaua București.
I won the League Cup with Middlesbrough in 2004 — though I made what was generously described as a 'howler' in the final.
I was the first non-Briton ever to make 500 Premier League appearances.
footballers.story: Middlesbrough's Australian wall for over a decade, then Fulham's, then a quiet medal-collecting spell at Chelsea in his forties. The most appearance-clocking non-British keeper the Premier League has ever seen — and a man who somehow kept playing top-flight football until he was 43.
footballers.numbers: 150+ Premier League clean sheets · first non-Briton to 500 PL appearances
didYouKnow: At 41, he became the oldest player ever to debut in the Champions League when Chelsea played Steaua București in December 2013 — kept a clean sheet too.
His 514 Premier League appearances make him the highest-appearing non-British player in the league's history.
His son Julian is also a goalkeeper and is eligible to represent the Philippines through his Filipino mother.

footballers.name: Morten Gamst Pedersen
clues: I was born on the 8th of September 1981. I played 83 times for my country, scoring 17 goals.
I have Sami heritage and once fronted a Norwegian footballer boyband called The Players.
My 2006 Euro qualifier goal against Hungary was so reminiscent of van Basten's 1988 final volley that the Norwegian media nicknamed me 'van Gamsten'.
I was at the centre of one of the Premier League's strangest scuffles when Mikel Arteta poked me in the eye after a tackle in 2010.
I spent nine years at Blackburn Rovers — famous for my left foot and dead-ball deliveries.
footballers.story: Blackburn's Norwegian left foot — nine years at Ewood Park, hundreds of inswinging deliveries, and a knack for the spectacular. Never the quickest, never the flashiest, but you knew exactly what you were getting: dead-ball danger and a left peg straight from the pages of an old coaching manual.
footballers.numbers: 118 goals in 633 club appearances · 17 in 83 for Norway
didYouKnow: His 2006 Euro qualifier goal against Hungary was so reminiscent of van Basten's 1988 final volley that the Norwegian media nicknamed him "van Gamsten".
Was at the centre of one of the Premier League's odder scuffles when Mikel Arteta poked him in the cheek and then the eye after a tackle in 2010.
Has Sami heritage and fronted a Norwegian footballer boyband called "The Players" — released a single for an anti-crime charity campaign.

footballers.name: Geovanni
clues: I was born on the 11th of January 1980. I played just once for my country, never scoring a goal.
I won the FIFA U-17 World Championship with Brazil in 1997 — also topped the scoring charts at the South American U-17.
I was San Jose Earthquakes' first ever MLS Designated Player.
I was twice my Portuguese club's Player of the Year, in 2004 and 2005.
I scored the first ever Premier League goal for my Yorkshire club on debut against Fulham in August 2008.
footballers.story: The Brazilian who scored Hull City's first ever Premier League goal — and looked, for one season, like the most exotic thing the KC Stadium had ever seen. Free-kick specialist, hip-swivelling dribbler, briefly a Barcelona player. Hull's all-time most unlikely cult hero.
footballers.numbers: 52 club goals in 341 appearances
didYouKnow: Scored Hull City's first ever Premier League goal on his debut against Fulham in August 2008 — also took Man of the Match.
Was twice Benfica's Player of the Year and curled in a famous late free-kick in the Lisbon derby that sealed Champions League qualification.
Signed by San Jose Earthquakes in 2010 as their first ever Designated Player — a brief but historically notable MLS stint.

footballers.name: Fabrizio Ravanelli
clues: I was born on the 11th of December 1968. I played 22 times for my country, scoring 8 goals.
My signature shirt-over-the-head celebration was so iconic that FIFA later rewrote the rules to make it a yellow-card offence.
I inherited my nickname 'The White Feather' from Juventus legend Roberto Bettega — both because of my prematurely white hair.
I scored a Premier League hat-trick on my debut for Middlesbrough against Liverpool, opening day of the 1996–97 season.
I won the Champions League with Juventus in 1996 — scored their goal in the final against Ajax in Rome.
footballers.story: The White Feather. Juventus's snarling Champions League winner who, in a piece of mid-90s football madness, turned up at Middlesbrough and scored a hat-trick on his Premier League debut. The shirt-over-the-head celebration belonged to him before it belonged to anyone else.
footballers.numbers: 190 club goals · 8 in 22 for Italy
didYouKnow: Nicknamed "the White Feather" because of his prematurely white hair — inherited the moniker from Juve legend Roberto Bettega.
Scored all five in Juve's 5–1 demolition of CSKA Sofia in September 1994.
His shirt-over-the-head celebration was so iconic that FIFA later changed the rules to make it a yellow-card offence.

footballers.name: Charlie Adam
clues: I was born on the 10th of December 1985. I played 26 times for my country, never scoring a goal.
My Stoke teammates secretly nicknamed me 'Parched' — I didn't find out for years until Peter Crouch revealed it on his podcast.
I scored a 65-yard goal against Chelsea in 2015 from inside my own half — described it as a once-in-a-lifetime strike.
I captained Dundee to promotion to the Scottish Premiership in 2021 — called it the highlight of my career, above Rangers Champions League nights.
I was the heart of Blackpool's 2010–11 Premier League season — then signed for Liverpool.
footballers.story: The Scottish set-piece merchant who carried Blackpool through their fairytale Premier League season, parlayed it into a Liverpool move, and ended up as Stoke's resident hammer-of-a-left-foot. Capable of moments of genius — and a sixty-five yarder against Chelsea.
footballers.numbers: 80 club goals · 26 caps for Scotland
didYouKnow: Scored a 65-yard goal against Chelsea in 2015 from inside his own half — called it a "once in a lifetime" strike.
His Stoke teammates secretly nicknamed him "Parched" — a long-running joke he didn't find out about for years until Peter Crouch revealed it on his podcast.
Captained Dundee to promotion in 2021 and called it the highlight of his career — above his Rangers Champions League nights.

footballers.name: Andrew Johnson
clues: I was born on the 10th of February 1981. I played 8 times for my country, never scoring a goal.
I revealed I could represent Poland through my grandfather mid-2004–05 — Sven-Göran Eriksson hurried up and gave me my England debut against the Netherlands two weeks later.
Mourinho publicly branded me 'untrustworthy' after a Chelsea-Everton game in 2006 — Everton threatened legal action and he apologised two days later.
I scored 21 Premier League goals in my debut top-flight season — second only to Thierry Henry.
I was voted into Crystal Palace's Centenary XI in 2005 — the only active player in the team.
footballers.story: The relentless Palace striker who outran Premier League defences for two seasons and very nearly carried Selhurst Park on his back. Quick, brave, fond of going down — depending on who you asked. Twenty-one league goals in his debut Premier League season at a relegated club.
honours: Premier League Player of the Month — Oct 2004 & Sep 2006
footballers.numbers: 114 club goals · 8 in 8 for England
didYouKnow: Was eligible for Poland through his grandfather — Sven-Göran Eriksson handed him an England debut almost immediately to prevent it.
Mourinho publicly branded him "untrustworthy" in 2006; the PGMOL later defended him as unfairly tarred with the diving brush.
Voted into Crystal Palace's Centenary XI in 2005 — the only active player in the team.

footballers.name: Rui Costa
clues: I was born on the 29th of March 1972. I played 94 times for my country, scoring 26 goals.
The only red card of my career came in 1997 — for walking off the pitch too slowly while being substituted in a World Cup qualifier against Germany. They equalised moments later.
I starred in Nike's 1996 'Good vs Evil' commercial alongside Ronaldo, Maldini and Cantona.
I won the Champions League with AC Milan at Old Trafford in 2003 — beating Juventus on penalties.
I've been the president of Benfica since 2021 — elected with 84% of the vote.
footballers.story: The understated Portuguese conductor of Fiorentina's late-90s side and Milan's Champions League winners. All grace, weight of pass, and quiet authority — the kind of number 10 who made everyone around him better and never quite got the credit for it.
footballers.numbers: 66 club goals · 26 in 94 for Portugal · Euro 2004 finalist
didYouKnow: The only red card of his entire career came in 1997 — for walking off the pitch too slowly during a World Cup qualifier, which cost Portugal qualification.
Starred in Nike's iconic 1996 "Good vs Evil" commercial alongside Ronaldo and Cantona — the Roman amphitheatre one.
Has been President of Benfica since October 2021, elected with 84% of the vote.

footballers.name: Iván Campo
clues: I was born on the 21st of February 1974. I played 4 times for my country, never scoring a goal.
I battled an anxiety disorder during my time at Real Madrid, undergoing therapy that I later credited with rescuing my career.
Sam Allardyce reckoned my long-range strike against Spurs in August 2006 had won Goal of the Season before the season had really started.
I was loaned to Bolton in 2003 expecting to return to Real Madrid — I unexpectedly made it permanent because I preferred living in Bolton.
I won the Champions League twice with Real Madrid — and played the full 90 minutes of the 2000 final against Valencia.
footballers.story: The wild-haired Spaniard who walked out of Real Madrid's Champions League winners' photo and into a Sam Allardyce midfield at Bolton — and looked, somehow, completely at home. A two-time European champion playing route one football in the rain at the Reebok.
footballers.numbers: 21 goals in 384 club appearances
didYouKnow: Sam Allardyce reckoned Campo's long-range strike against Spurs in August 2006 had won Goal of the Season before the season had really started.
Battled an anxiety disorder during his Madrid years, undergoing therapy that he later credited with rescuing his career.
Loaned to Bolton in 2003 expecting to return to Madrid — instead signed permanently because he simply preferred living in Bolton.

footballers.name: Jason Koumas
clues: I was born on the 25th of September 1979. I played 34 times for my country, scoring 10 goals.
My son Lewis came through the same academy I did and made his senior debut for the club in 2024.
I was the Championship Player of the Year in 2006–07 at West Brom.
I came through the Liverpool academy alongside Steven Gerrard and Michael Owen — I left because they wouldn't play me centrally.
The Guardian once argued my talent was no less than Gerrard's — and put my career down to being undisciplined.
footballers.story: The Welsh playmaker whose feet were fluent in a language his career never quite spoke. Mercurial, brilliant on his day, infuriatingly anonymous on others. The Championship's outstanding talent of the mid-2000s — he should have done more, and everyone knew it.
honours: PFA Team of the Year × 4 (between 2001–02 and 2006–07)
Cardiff City Player of the Year — 2005–06
footballers.numbers: 68 goals in 422 club appearances · 10 in 34 for Wales
didYouKnow: Came through the Liverpool academy alongside Steven Gerrard and Michael Owen — left because they wouldn't play him centrally.
The Guardian once argued his raw talent was "no less than Gerrard's" and blamed his career on being "undisciplined".
His son Lewis came through the Liverpool academy and made his senior debut for the club in February 2024.

footballers.name: Marc Overmars
clues: I was born on the 29th of March 1973. I played 86 times for my country, scoring 17 goals.
I announced my £25m transfer on my own personal website before telling the media — it generated 250,000 hits in a single day.
I resigned as Ajax's director of football in 2022 after admitting to sending inappropriate messages to female colleagues.
I completely tore my cruciate ligament in 1995 and came back to anchor a Premier League and FA Cup Double-winning Arsenal side.
I was a flying Dutch winger for Ajax, Arsenal and Barcelona — and a Champions League winner with Ajax in 1995.
footballers.story: Lightning-quick Dutch winger who tore up three leagues in three countries. Ajax's Champions League hero, Arsenal's double-winning game-changer, and one of Barcelona's first marquee Premier League imports. Blew his ACL clean off the bone in 1995 and somehow came back faster.
footballers.numbers: 78 club goals · 17 in 86 for the Netherlands
didYouKnow: Suffered a completely severed cruciate ligament in 1995 and recovered to anchor Arsenal's 1997–98 Double-winning side.
Announced his £25m move to Barcelona on his personal website before telling the press — got 250,000 hits in a day, an internet-era footballing first.
Resigned as Ajax's director of football in 2022 after admitting to sending inappropriate messages to female colleagues; signed with Antwerp weeks later amid sponsor backlash.

footballers.name: Jay-Jay Okocha
clues: I was born on the 14th of August 1973. I played 73 times for my country, scoring 14 goals.
While at Fenerbahçe I took Turkish citizenship and adopted the playing name 'Muhammed Yavuz' — a tribute to a club staff member I admired.
I won an Olympic gold medal in 1996, beating Brazil in the semi-final and Argentina in the final.
I won the African Cup of Nations in 1994 and went on to play for Eintracht Frankfurt and Paris Saint-Germain.
My nickname at Bolton was so iconic the club printed it on shirts: 'so good they named him twice'.
footballers.story: So good they named him twice. The Nigerian magician who turned the Reebok Stadium into a circus tent — flicks, rabonas, no-look passes — and somehow kept Bolton in the Premier League while doing it. The patron saint of unnecessary skill.
footballers.numbers: 89 club goals · 14 in 73 for Nigeria
didYouKnow: Took Turkish citizenship at Fenerbahçe and adopted the playing name "Muhammed Yavuz" in tribute to a club staff member.
Mentored a young Ronaldinho at PSG, who later credited him as a stylistic influence.
The "so good they named him twice" line wasn't a slogan — Bolton printed it on the back of replica shirts.

footballers.name: Ricardo Carvalho
clues: I was born on the 18th of May 1978. I played 89 times for my country, scoring 5 goals.
Jose Mourinho once suggested I should take an IQ test, after I publicly questioned why he hadn't started me in a season opener.
I was named UEFA Club Defender of the Year in 2003–04.
I've been managed by Jose Mourinho at three different clubs.
I was stamped on by Wayne Rooney at the 2006 World Cup — he was sent off for it.
footballers.story: The most underrated centre-back of his generation. Won the Champions League with Porto, the Premier League three times with Chelsea, La Liga with Real Madrid — and Euro 2016 with Portugal at 38. Wherever Mourinho went, Carvalho followed.
footballers.numbers: 19 club goals in 433 appearances · 89 caps for Portugal
didYouKnow: Followed José Mourinho from Porto to Chelsea in 2004, then again from Inter to Real Madrid in 2010 — one of the great manager-player partnerships of the era.
Retired from international football in 2011 saying he'd been "disrespected", then came back nearly three years later and won Euro 2016 with Portugal at 38.
Was given a seven-month suspended prison sentence in Spain in 2017 for tax fraud during his Real Madrid years — a fate that befell several Spain-based stars of his era.

footballers.name: Cheick Tioté
clues: I was born on the 21st of June 1986. I played 52 times for my country, scoring 1 goal.
I represented my country at the 2010 and 2014 World Cups, and the 2012 and 2013 Africa Cup of Nations.
I scored arguably the most famous goal in my club’s modern history — a stunning volley to make it 4–4 against Arsenal, completing a comeback from 4–0 down.
I played alongside Yohan Cabaye and Hatem Ben Arfa in a midfield that gave Newcastle their best Premier League finish in years.
I tragically collapsed and died during a training session in June 2017 at the age of 30.

footballers.name: John Arne Riise
clues: I was born on the 24th of September 1980. I played 110 times for my country, scoring 16 goals.

I left Ligue 1 as a relatively unknown 20-year-old, but within months of arriving in England I’d already announced myself with one of the most spectacular debut goals the Premier League had ever seen.
A teammate once attacked me with a golf club on a pre-season training camp in Portugal — the story became one of football’s most bizarre dressing-room incidents.
I was part of a squad that came back from 3–0 down to win the biggest trophy in European club football on a famous night in Turkey.
I was known for having one of the most powerful left foots in football history — one of my strikes was reportedly clocked at over 70 miles per hour.
